main_summary.summary: Sam Young, Jr., born March 13, 1950 in Worth County (Sylvester), Georgia, recounts growing up on a Black family farm under Freedom of Choice school integration, witnessing racial violence in his community, and his work with the Southwest Georgia Project for Community Education and New Communities Inc. He describes his father as politically engaged, exposing the children to civil rights news from a young age. Young recalls the vandalism of Blue Spring Baptist Church, the arrest of two cousins from a Worth County High School bus and their three-day incommunicado detention until attorney C.B. King intervened, and the shotgun assault on neighbor C.T. Rockwell by a white employer over the title 'Mister.' Graduating high school in 1968, he briefly worked construction at a Firestone Tire site before joining the Southwest Georgia Project, where Charles Sherrod, Robert Mants, and Randolph Battle became mentors. He helped operate the New Communities Inc collective land project after Sherrod located the tract, lived on-site with his family beginning in 1974, and farmed peanuts, corn, and vegetables until ASCS loan foreclosures shut the farm down in the 1970s. He describes the Pigford v. Glickman class action that finally produced settlement payments in 2009 as restitution for federal discrimination against Black farmers.
key_themes: Southwest Georgia Project for Community Education
New Communities Inc collective land project
USDA / ASCS discrimination against Black farmers
main_summary.historical_significance:
Young's account is a ground-level participant testimony from New Communities Inc, the collective Black-owned land project that Charles Sherrod and Shirley Sherrod organized in Southwest Georgia in 1968, widely regarded as a precursor model for Black community land trusts. His description of how ASCS loan foreclosures dismantled the project in the 1970s, and how the Pigford class-action settlement only materialized in 2009, anchors the federal-discrimination claim in lived experience rather than legal abstraction.

He also preserves a local-history record of incidents that did not enter the national civil rights narrative: the Blue Spring Baptist Church vandalism, the Worth County High School arrests and C.B. King's intervention to free the detained cousins, and the C.T. Rockwell shooting over the refusal to address a white teenager as 'Mister.' These details document the routine of racial violence in rural Worth County and the role of Albany-based Black attorneys as the legal defense infrastructure for the surrounding counties.

chapters.title: Worth County Childhood
chapters.summary: Sam Young, Jr. introduces himself as born March 13, 1950 in Worth County, Sylvester, Georgia. He describes his father, Sam Young, Sr., as politically engaged and committed to keeping the family informed about civil rights developments, including Brown v. Board of Education, well before Young could understand them.

chapters.title: Freedom of Choice Integration
chapters.summary: Young describes the Freedom of Choice school-integration regime, the daily walk to segregated schools alongside roads where white school buses carried students who shouted racial slurs and where Black children responded by throwing rocks. He recalls turpentine-time work in the surrounding pine woods, his father holding him back to repeat second grade so he and his sister could go through school together, and the strict Black teachers who tolerated no cheating.

chapters.title: Racial Violence in Worth County
chapters.summary: Young recounts three incidents that shaped his understanding of racial terror: the vandalism of Blue Spring Baptist Church in Worth Church, painted black and labeled 'the devil's den' by local white youth; the arrest of his two cousins from a Worth County High School bus and the sheriff's three-day refusal to disclose their whereabouts until attorney C.B. King threatened the sheriff with arrest; and the shotgun assault on C.T. Rockwell by white employer Albert Gill, triggered by Rockwell's refusal to call Gill's teenage son 'Mister.'

chapters.title: Family Farm and ASCS Foreclosures
chapters.summary: Young describes growing up the oldest son in a family of ten sisters and seven brothers on a Worth County farm, planting cotton, corn, peanuts, squash, butter beans, peas, and okra. His father's farm loans were foreclosed and the family was sustained only by a local white grocer who extended a personal line of credit. Young identifies the ASCS (Agricultural Stabilization and Conservation Service), now Farm Service Agency, as the federal mechanism that foreclosed on Black farmers in the area.

chapters.title: Firestone and Entry to SWGP
chapters.summary: Young graduates from Worth County High School in 1968 as the only male in a class of about five. His first job was at the Firestone Tire construction site in Albany, where supervisors were harsh and he was assigned to break up concrete foundations with hand tools. Let go along with other workers, he learned from his two older sisters about the Southwest Georgia Project for Community Education and joined as a young recruit.

chapters.title: Sherrod, Mants, and Battle as Mentors
chapters.summary: Young identifies his three mentors in the Southwest Georgia Project: Charles Sherrod, Robert Mants, and Randolph Battle. He describes attending Albany Technical College for graphic arts on a SWGP scholarship to learn printing-press operation in service of a community newspaper. He also names attorney Mary Young as a major champion of civil rights cases in the Albany area, who began her practice with C.B. King.

chapters.title: New Communities Inc Land Project
chapters.summary: Young describes Charles Sherrod's New York research trip that located the large tract of land for sale in Southwest Georgia, and the project's purpose: a collective Black-owned land base for community self-sufficiency. The land was secured in 1968. Young married in 1974 and moved his wife and children onto the site, where he operated farm activities. The site had over 300 acres of peanut allotment plus corn, okra, watermelons, and other vegetables, and existing dwellings that housed multiple Black families and outside students.

chapters.title: Pigford Lawsuit and Land Loss
chapters.summary: Young describes the class-action suit in which the federal government finally admitted ASCS discrimination against Black farmers. The suit was filed sometime in the late 1980s or mid-1990s, but the final settlement payment did not come through until 2009. By then New Communities Inc had been disqualified as a farm and lost its land. Shirley and Charles Sherrod used settlement funds to purchase a new tract of land in (likely) Dougherty County to continue the project's work.

chapters.title: Reflection and Synectics Training
chapters.summary: Young reflects that being part of the Southwest Georgia Project gave him access to travel and training that other rural Georgia youth never received. He describes a Synectics problem-solving and communication-skills training in New York where participants were recorded on camera while developing group solutions to scenarios. He closes by urging that issues be addressed in the moment rather than deferred, and by offering his testimony as one small piece of the larger Movement story.

loc_video.caption: Sam Young, Jr., oral history interview conducted by Will Griffin in Albany, Georgia, 2013 March 09
